reasoning: The PKGBUILD's dynamic checksum fetching from archive.torproject.org is non-standard but not malicious. The tarball is verified via PGP signatures (validpgpkeys) and all executable scripts are standard Tor Browser components. Qwen's network access concern is valid but mitigated by Tor Project's domain reputation and PGP verification.
disagreement_analysis: Qwen overreacted to dynamic checksum fetching in PKGBUILD parsing, while DeepSeek performed comprehensive file review confirming all components are legitimate Tor Browser artifacts.
coverage_issues: Qwen failed to review critical files: tor-browser.install, start-tor-browser script, and wrapper scripts that execute during installation/runtime
false_positives: Qwen's PKGBUILD verdict: Dynamic checksum fetch from archive.torproject.org isn't ideal but doesn't bypass PGP verification or introduce execution risk
false_negatives: DeepSeek didn't flag PKGBUILD's dynamic checksum fetch as non-standard (hygiene issue), though not security-critical
learnings: File selection: Models must review ALL executable scripts/hooks even if PKGBUILD has anomalies
Assessment quality: Improve context awareness - network access to primary upstream isn't equivalent to arbitrary remote code execution
Failure modes: Overweighting build-time anomalies while ignoring runtime component analysis leads to false positives
